title: Jamaican fish tea
subtitle: Light spiced fish broth with yam, potato, and scotch bonnet
excerpt: A clear, lightly spiced Jamaican fish broth served as a restorative — despite the name, it is a soup. Poached fish with sweet potato, carrot, and spring onion in a scotch bonnet and allspice broth.
sourceNotes: Jamaican fish tea is a clear, light fish broth served at roadside cookshops, markets, and at parties as a restorative or hangover remedy. Despite the name, it is a light soup rather than a tea. Traditional versions use whole fish (red snapper, parrot fish, or sprat) with yam and green banana. This recipe uses cod fillet and sweet potato as accessible substitutes. The scotch bonnet floats whole in the broth to infuse heat without piercing — the amount of heat is controlled by how long it stays in.

content.text: Fish tea is a Jamaican institution that doesn't translate well to description: a light fish broth, ladled into a cup, drunk standing up at a market stall. The name is the joke: it looks like tea, it is drunk like tea, and it does what tea is supposed to do — revive you. In practice it is a clean, spiced fish broth with a little body from the root vegetables, and it is much more useful than the name suggests.

The whole scotch bonnet floats in the broth rather than being chopped in; it perfumes the broth with its heat without releasing everything it has. Take it out when the heat reaches the level you want. The fish goes in last and poaches gently — it needs only 5 minutes and should not be overcooked.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into chunks — yam if available

content.text: Put the {{sweet-potato}} sweet potato, {{potato}} potatoes, {{carrot}} carrots, spring onion bundle, whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and {{allspice}} of allspice into a large saucepan. Pour over 1.5 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil over medium heat, then reduce to a simmer. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 20 minutes until the root vegetables are tender.

Remove the spring onion bundle and thyme sprigs. Taste the broth — if you want more heat, leave the scotch bonnet in for 5 more minutes, then remove. Add the {{cod-fillet}} pieces of cod to the simmering broth. Poach for 5 minutes, just until the fish is opaque and flakes easily. Do not stir after adding the fish, or it will break apart. Ladle carefully into bowls, making sure each serving gets some fish and vegetables.

items.cause: Not enough aromatics; too much water
items.fix: Add another spring onion bundle and another sprig of thyme. Simmer uncovered for 10 more minutes to concentrate the flavour. A pinch more allspice helps.
Add the fish only in the last 5 minutes. Do not stir once it is in — let it poach undisturbed and use a ladle to transfer to bowls.

items.cause: Scotch bonnet pierced or left in too long
items.fix: Remove the scotch bonnet as soon as the heat level suits you. Add a splash of cold water and a squeeze of lime to cool it down.

items.symptom: Sweet potato disintegrating into the broth
items.cause: Cut too small; cooked too long
items.fix: Cut root vegetables into larger chunks (5 cm). Add the sweet potato 10 minutes after the potato and carrot, since it cooks faster.

content.text: Fish tea occupies a particular place in Jamaican food culture: it is the restorative, the breakfast broth, the thing you drink after a long night or a rough morning. Vendors sell it from pots at market stalls and road-side spots, ladled into styrofoam cups. In the home kitchen it is a quick lunch or a first course before something more substantial. The combination of clean fish stock, allspice, and the sweet heat of scotch bonnet is distinctly Jamaican and quite unlike anything in European broth-cooking.
